title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: , estimate logistic regression 1000000 observations 50 covariates. vary number cores used nthreads argument parglm.control. method argument sets method used. LINPACK uses pivoted QR (dqrdc2) glm.fit final decomposition; LAPACK uses DGEQP3 LAPACK throughout; FAST solves normal equations Fisher information, similar speedglm package. plot shows median run times versus number cores. Coloured horizontal lines show single-threaded reference times glm, speedglm, glm2, fastglm, bigglm, bam. used glm.fit parglm.fit. make relative difference bigger call e.g., model.matrix model.frame take time. show point, first compute much time takes make plot. black solid line computation time model.matrix model.frame. Plot runtime versus number cores. open circles FAST method filled circles LINPACK method. FAST method, speedglm, fastglm, fastglm (LDLT) compute Fisher information solve normal equation; fastglm (LDLT) uses LDLT Cholesky decomposition (method 3) rather default column-pivoted QR (method 0). advantage terms computation cost may lead unstable solutions. can alter number observations parallel chunk block_size argument parglm.control. single threaded performance parglm may slower coefficients. cause seems difference LAPACK LINPACK implementation. presumably due either QR decomposition method /qr.qty method. Windows, parglm seem slower built Rtools reason seems qr.qty method LAPACK, dormqr, slower LINPACK method, dqrsl. illustration computation times machine.
code: ##### # simulate n # number of observations #> [1] 1000000 p # number of covariates #> [1] 50 set.seed(68024947) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, 1/p, 1/sqrt(p)), n, ncol = p) df <- data.frame(y = 1/(1 + exp(-(rowSums(X) - 1))) > runif(n), X) ##### # compute and measure time. Setup call to make library(microbenchmark) library(speedglm) #> Loading required package: Matrix #> Loading required package: MASS #> Loading required package: biglm #> Loading required package: DBI library(fastglm) library(glm2) #>  #> Attaching package: 'glm2' #> The following object is masked from 'package:MASS': #>  #>     crabs library(biglm) library(mgcv) #> Loading required package: nlme #> This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'. #>  #> Attaching package: 'mgcv' #> The following object is masked from 'package:fastglm': #>  #>     negbin library(parglm) X_mat <- model.matrix(y ~ ., df) biglm_form <- reformulate(names(df)[-1], response = "y") bam_form   <- biglm_form cl <- list(   quote(microbenchmark),   glm      = quote(glm     (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   speedglm = quote(speedglm(y ~ ., family = binomial(), data = df)),   glm2     = quote(glm2    (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   fastglm  = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial())),   fastglm3 = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial(), method = 3L)),   bigglm   = quote(bigglm  (biglm_form, df, family = binomial())),   bam      = quote(bam     (bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df)),   times = 11L) tfunc <- function(method = "LINPACK", nthreads)   parglm(y ~ ., binomial(), df, control = parglm.control(method = method,                                                          nthreads = nthreads)) cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(nthreads = .(i))))) names(cl)[10:(10L + n_threads - 1L)] <- paste0("parglm-LINPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "FAST")))) names(cl)[(10L + n_threads):(10L + 2L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-FAST-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "LAPACK")))) names(cl)[(10L + 2L * n_threads):(10L + 3L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-LAPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- as.call(cl) cl # the call we make #> microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~  #>     ., family = binomial(), data = df), glm2 = glm2(y ~ ., binomial(),  #>     df), fastglm = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial()),  #>     fastglm3 = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial(),  #>         method = 3L), bigglm = bigglm(biglm_form, df, family = binomial()),  #>     bam = bam(bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df), times = 11L,  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L), `parglm-LINPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L), `parglm-LINPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L), `parglm-LINPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L), `parglm-LINPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L), `parglm-LINPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L),  #>     `parglm-FAST-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L, method = "LAPACK"))  out <- eval(cl) #> Warning in microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = #> speedglm(y ~ : less accurate nanosecond times to avoid potential integer #> overflows s <- summary(out) # result from `microbenchmark` print(s[, c("expr", "min", "mean", "median", "max")], digits  = 3,       row.names = FALSE) #>               expr  min mean median  max #>                glm 2419 2611   2527 2998 #>           speedglm  786  896    853 1281 #>               glm2 2445 2668   2641 3005 #>            fastglm 1934 2068   2033 2436 #>           fastglm3  559  596    582  699 #>             bigglm 6325 6625   6540 7465 #>                bam 5060 5229   5175 5918 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1 1857 1904   1891 2027 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2 1647 1708   1692 1905 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3 1596 1663   1613 1952 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4 1561 1666   1658 1969 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5 1406 1509   1442 1848 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6 1387 1466   1442 1650 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7 1394 1479   1416 1822 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8 1376 1487   1431 1831 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9 1366 1449   1428 1644 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10 1364 1451   1416 1716 #>      parglm-FAST-1  501  575    572  679 #>      parglm-FAST-2  399  457    443  535 #>      parglm-FAST-3  376  437    420  508 #>      parglm-FAST-4  381  454    423  618 #>      parglm-FAST-5  356  537    430  892 #>      parglm-FAST-6  350  421    410  570 #>      parglm-FAST-7  369  401    399  483 #>      parglm-FAST-8  349  424    399  630 #>      parglm-FAST-9  358  424    409  553 #>     parglm-FAST-10  351  402    389  540 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1 1846 1914   1873 2037 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2 1657 1723   1724 1792 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3 1584 1673   1626 1860 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4 1555 1641   1645 1782 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5 1414 1482   1463 1698 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6 1394 1446   1418 1649 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7 1394 1472   1421 1577 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8 1376 1473   1420 1776 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9 1371 1450   1446 1581 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10 1388 1443   1405 1764 modmat_time <- microbenchmark(   modmat_time = {     mf <- model.frame(y ~ ., df); model.matrix(terms(mf), mf)   }, times = 10) modmat_time # time taken by `model.matrix` and `model.frame` #> Unit: milliseconds #>         expr       min        lq        mean     median         uq        max #>  modmat_time 93.199765 96.711579 113.3256072 114.322883 129.492432 140.169242 #>  neval #>     10 par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, .5, 9)) o <- aggregate(time ~ expr, out, median)[, 2] / 10^9 ylim <- range(o, 0); ylim[2] <- ylim[2] + .04 * diff(ylim)  o_linpack <- o[8L:(n_threads + 7L)] o_fast    <- o[(n_threads + 8L):(2L * n_threads + 7L)] o_lapack  <- o[(2L * n_threads + 8L):(3L * n_threads + 7L)]  ref_cols <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#0072B2", "#F0E442", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7") ref_lty  <- c("dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", "longdash", "44", "twodash", "1373")  plot(1:n_threads, o_linpack, xlab = "Number of cores", yaxs = "i",      ylim = ylim, ylab = "Run time (s)", pch = 16, cex = 1.4) points(1:n_threads, o_fast,   pch = 1, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) points(1:n_threads, o_lapack, pch = 4, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) for (i in 1:7)   abline(h = o[i], lty = ref_lty[i], col = ref_cols[i], lwd = 2) abline(h = median(modmat_time$time) / 10^9, lty = "solid", col = "black",        lwd = 2) usr <- par("usr") par(xpd = TRUE) legend(x = usr[2], y = usr[4], xjust = 0, yjust = 1,        legend = c("glm", "speedglm", "glm2", "fastglm", "fastglm (LDLT)",                   "bigglm", "bam", "model.matrix", "parglm LINPACK",                   "parglm FAST", "parglm LAPACK"),        lty = c(ref_lty, "solid", NA, NA, NA),        lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA),        col = c(ref_cols, "black", "black", "black", "black"),        pch = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 16, 1, 4),        bty = "n") qr1 <- qr(X) qr2 <- qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   `qr LINPACK`     = qr(X),   `qr LAPACK`      = qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE),   `qr.qty LINPACK` = qr.qty(qr1, df$y),   `qr.qty LAPACK`  = qr.qty(qr2, df$y),   times = 11) #> Unit: milliseconds #>            expr        min          lq         mean     median          uq #>      qr LINPACK 413.159091 418.9232195 424.91441718 421.257698 423.5853090 #>       qr LAPACK 367.659997 368.7497975 379.22206409 370.791577 374.1127820 #>  qr.qty LINPACK  46.415362  47.3859755  60.31240145  48.372620  57.5113150 #>   qr.qty LAPACK 111.620901 111.8113255 113.09574927 112.622285 112.8296835 #>         max neval #>  448.465052    11 #>  460.526432    11 #>  130.116206    11 #>  117.830884    11

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: smaller datasets per-call overhead parglm’s thread pool can exceed gains parallelism. repeat benchmark n = 100,000 n = 10,000, dropping bam since aimed much larger problems. Note default block_size parglm.control 10000, n = 10,000 work split across threads parglm effectively runs single-threaded regardless nthreads.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 10,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: per-row cost method scales roughly \(p^2\) (computing \(X'WX\) IRLS iteration), parglm’s thread-pool overhead roughly fixed per call. two effects compete: small \(p\) overhead dominates parallel benefit, larger \(p\) parallel work amortizes overhead parglm pulls ahead. contrast n = 100,000, p = 5 (overhead wins) n = 1,000,000, p = 20 (parallel work wins).

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000 p = 5.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 1,000,000 p = 20.
code: invisible(run_and_plot(n = 1000000L, p = 20L, n_threads = n_threads)) #>               expr  min mean median  max #>                glm  956  975    968 1014 #>           speedglm  526  564    558  612 #>               glm2  977 1088   1037 1610 #>            fastglm  534  559    555  604 #>     fastglm (LDLT)  266  285    271  348 #>             bigglm 2502 2627   2600 2821 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1  561  578    575  604 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2  478  494    487  545 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3  458  496    480  566 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4  443  470    465  542 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5  424  467    444  570 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6  419  458    435  600 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7  432  498    495  587 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8  415  439    436  479 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9  407  438    424  547 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10  405  425    424  463 #>      parglm-FAST-1  366  395    392  474 #>      parglm-FAST-2  295  330    317  503 #>      parglm-FAST-3  276  306    296  376 #>      parglm-FAST-4  269  287    287  319 #>      parglm-FAST-5  268  300    279  384 #>      parglm-FAST-6  264  299    284  419 #>      parglm-FAST-7  255  290    282  362 #>      parglm-FAST-8  256  272    273  292 #>      parglm-FAST-9  259  305    286  436 #>     parglm-FAST-10  251  296    276  430 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1  563  585    573  646 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2  476  498    495  546 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3  464  482    472  512 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4  445  468    459  554 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5  423  459    444  565 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6  421  458    448  573 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7  416  435    432  469 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8  411  437    427  510 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9  414  470    445  583 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10  406  424    411  467

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: simulate Poisson dataset 20 clusters 10 observations , cluster-level random effect induces within-cluster correlation.
code: library(parglm) library(sandwich) library(lmtest) set.seed(1) n          <- 200 cluster_id <- rep(1:20, each = 10) x1         <- rnorm(n) x2         <- rnorm(n) u          <- rep(rnorm(20, sd = 0.5), each = 10)  # cluster random effect y          <- rpois(n, exp(0.5 + 0.3 * x1 - 0.2 * x2 + u)) dat        <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, cluster = cluster_id)

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
code: fit <- parglm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, family = poisson(),               control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L))

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: default model-based standard errors assume Poisson variance equals mean. small cluster random effects induce overdispersion.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: vcovHC() computes sandwich standard errors robust misspecification variance function. HC3 (default) recommended small moderate samples.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: vcovCL() accounts within-cluster correlation, appropriate correction . expected, cluster-robust standard errors larger model-based ones, reflecting extra variability due cluster random effects.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: covariance matrices also available:

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package
text: model = TRUE (default parglm) must set model frame stored, allowing sandwich reconstruct design matrix internally.

title: Parallel GLM
text: parglm package provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS. can install release version CRAN install development version GitHub calling: See vignette example run times details.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS.

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Maintainer: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Authors: Tom Palmer remlapmot@hotmail.com (ORCID) Benjamin Christoffersen boennecd@gmail.com (ORCID) contributors: Anthony Williams [copyright holder] Boost developers [copyright holder]

what: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Auxiliary function parglm fitting.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: parglm.control(   epsilon = 1e-08,   maxit = 25,   trace = FALSE,   nthreads = parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L),   block_size = NULL,   method = "LINPACK",   nthreads_auto = missing(nthreads) )

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: epsilon positive convergence tolerance. maxit integer giving maximal number IWLS iterations. trace logical indicating output produced estimation. nthreads number cores use. Defaults parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L), leaves one core free. may get best performance using available physical cores data set sufficiently large. block_size number observations include parallel block. method string specifying method use. Either "LINPACK", "LAPACK", "FAST". nthreads_auto logical; internal use . Records whether nthreads auto-detected (suppresses thread-reduction warning dataset small). set argument directly.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: list components named arguments.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: LINPACK method uses QR method glm.fit final QR decomposition. dqrdc2 method described qr. QR decompositions last made DGEQP3 LAPACK. See Wood, Goude, Shaw (2015) details QR method. FAST method computes Fisher information solves normal equation. faster less numerically stable.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Wood, S.N., Goude, Y. & Shaw S. (2015) Generalized additive models large datasets. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series C 64(1): 139-155.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: # use one core f1 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  # use two cores (mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: Function like glm can make computation parallel. function supports families listed family. See "vignette("parglm", "parglm")" run time examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: parglm(   formula,   family = gaussian,   data,   weights,   subset,   na.action,   start = NULL,   offset,   control = list(...),   contrasts = NULL,   model = TRUE,   x = FALSE,   y = TRUE,   ... )  parglm.fit(   x,   y,   weights = rep(1, NROW(x)),   start = NULL,   etastart = NULL,   mustart = NULL,   offset = rep(0, NROW(x)),   family = gaussian(),   control = list(),   intercept = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: formula object class formula. family family object. data optional data frame, list environment containing variables model. weights optional vector 'prior weights' used fitting process. NULL numeric vector. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs. start starting values parameters linear predictor. offset can used specify priori known component included linear predictor fitting. control list parameters controlling fitting process. parglm.fit passed parglm.control. contrasts optional list. See contrasts.arg model.matrix.default. model logical value indicating whether model frame included component returned value. x, y parglm: logical values indicating whether response vector model matrix used fitting process returned components returned value. parglm.fit: x design matrix dimension n * p, y vector observations length n. ... parglm: arguments used form default control argument supplied directly. parglm.fit: unused. etastart starting values linear predictor. supported. mustart starting values vector means. supported. intercept logical. intercept included null model?

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: glm object returned glm differs mainly qr element. qr element object returned parglm(.fit) \(R\) matrix QR decomposition.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: current implementation uses min(.integer(n / p), nthreads) threads n number observations, p number covariates, nthreads nthreads element list returned parglm.control. Thus, likely little () reduction computation time p almost equal n. current implementation handle p > n. Since parglm returns standard glm object, compatible sandwich package heteroskedasticity-consistent (HC) cluster-robust standard errors via vcovHC vcovCL. requires model = TRUE (default). See vignette("sandwich", "parglm") examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: # mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads (>= 16 rows per thread) f1 <- glm   (mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

title: parglm (development version)
text: Add biglm, fastglm, glm2, mgcv timing comparison vignette. add timings fewer observations fewer coefficients. Bump roxygen2 8.0.0 Remove busy-wait polling qr_parallel::get_stacks_res Return eta mu parallelglm skip R-side recomputation Sum upper triangle per-chunk Fisher information FAST path Drop const R_F members enable move semantics Hoist NA-zeroing beta per-chunk workers Use thread_local index thread_pool::get_id Skip intermediate matrix reverse-pivoting R Cache parglm_supported keys avoid rebuilding family objects per fit Change default parglm.control(nthreads) parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L) make better use available cores Implement persistent thread pool Replace linked-list task queue deque single mutex Fuse X chunk copy weight scaling one memory pass Support quasibinomial() quasipoisson() family specification Enable full compatibility sandwich package robust standard errors, including adding comments parglm helpfile, new vignette, tests Add fastglm’s method = 3L benchmarks parglm vignette Allow two column response family binomial quasibinomial Fix issue order summary.glm Warn rather error starting values found

title: parglm 0.1.8
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-21 Remove specification C++11 Amend std::result_of std::invoke_result header file Fix infinite recursion QR_base::qyt removing std::move Require C++17 Linux, macOS, Windows New maintainer - Tom Palmer

title: parglm 0.1.7
text: CRAN release: 2021-10-14 Avoid virtual function calls remove macros. Fix issue due new STRICT_R_HEADERS variable Rcpp.

title: parglm 0.1.6
text: CRAN release: 2020-08-10 Avoid memory allocations. Fix test issue ATLAS.

title: parglm 0.1.4
text: CRAN release: 2020-01-07 stops variables observations. Previously, caused crash. Handle Fortran string length argument.

title: parglm 0.1.3
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-18 Fix bug found Valgrind.

title: parglm 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2019-03-14 Minor changes implementation. Fix bugs patched R oldrel R.

title: parglm 0.1.1
text: CRAN release: 2019-01-19 FAST method added computes Fisher information solves normal equation speedglm. One change decreased computation time. Minor bug fixes.
